contest.title: 2024 ICPC Asia Taichung Regional Contest (Unrated, Online Mirror, ICPC Rules, Preferably Teams)

sources.notes: Read the complete statement and constraints: four-neighbor moves, walls, at most three consecutive moves in one direction, and n*m <= 200000.
Read the official contest analysis linked from Codeforces. It models vertices as maze positions augmented by the last direction and current consecutive-run length, then applies BFS.
Read the complete Luogu solution article and implementation. It independently confirms BFS over (cell,last_direction,run_length) and O(nm) time and memory.
Verified that the augmented state is Markovian, that all legal moves are represented exactly once as unit edges, and that ordinary BFS therefore returns the minimum legal number of steps.

annotation.summary: Find the shortest wall-avoiding path from S to T in a grid when no direction may be used for more than three consecutive steps.
annotation.constraints: 12 <= n*m <= 200000; 3 <= n,m <= 10000; exactly one S and T; the border is all walls. A constant 12 movement-history states per open cell fits comfortably.
annotation.core_idea: A cell alone is not a sufficient shortest-path state because future legal moves depend on how it was entered. Use states (cell,last_direction,run_length), where run_length is 1, 2, or 3. A move in a different direction resets the length to 1; a move in the same direction is allowed only when the length is below 3. Start from a sentinel no-direction state, run BFS on this unweighted augmented graph, and take the minimum distance among all states located at T.
annotation.complexity: O(12*n*m) time and O(12*n*m) memory, both O(nm), since every state is visited once and has at most four outgoing moves.
tricks: Do not mark only the grid cell visited; arrivals with different last directions or run lengths have different legal continuations.
Use a sentinel direction and run length 0 at S, or seed all legal first moves.
Because every transition costs one step, a FIFO queue is sufficient; Dijkstra and DFS are unnecessary.
The answer is the minimum over all direction/run states at T, not the first geometric visit under a cell-only visited array.

solution_variants.name: augmented-state-bfs
solution_variants.summary: Run BFS on up to twelve copies of every open cell, indexed by the last move direction and its current run length, rejecting only a fourth identical consecutive move.
solution_variants.complexity: O(nm) time and O(nm) memory with a constant factor of 12.

tags.evidence: Every legal move is a unit-weight edge in the augmented state graph, so BFS levels are exactly legal path lengths and the first distance assigned to a state is optimal.
The last direction and its run length must be added to the cell coordinate; without them the set of legal next moves is not determined by the state.
Each grid cell is represented by twelve direction/run layers, with movement edges between the appropriate layers.
The requested minimum number of legal steps is an unweighted shortest path in the augmented graph.
